segments.s734-g9: This is an adverbial phrase giving manner and direction: نهانی (an adverb) 'secretly' modifies the following verbal action; به گفتی uses به + a noun (گفتی 'words/conversation') to mean 'in/with words' or 'by speaking'; بگوش اندرون is a prepositional phrase 'into the inner ear' (بـ + گوش = 'to the ear', اندرون = 'inside/within'). Word order places these modifiers before the verb for emphasis (typical in poetic/classical Persian).
segments.s734-g10: همی‌خواندی is a classical/perfective-habitual verbal form with the prefix همی- marking continuous or habitual action (here past habitual: 'would recite/sing'); آشکارا 'openly' is an adverb qualifying how the recitation was done; برون 'out/outside' gives the spatial contrast to اندرون in the previous segment. The verb comes before these adverbials (poetic ordering) but governs them as its complements.
segments.s734-g11: Here the time/place phrase 'بگوش اندرون' ('to the inner ear') precedes the verb, then خواند (past, 3rd-singular) gives the action 'recited/spoke'. The proper name خسرو قباد follows the verb (a flexible, literary word order: V + subject). The verb خواند is used in the sense of 'recite/read aloud' rather than 'call'.
segments.s734-g12: همی‌گفت is the habitual/past iterative form 'kept saying' (همی- + past stem). The following nominal phrase is a predicate: شیر 'a lion' (used metaphorically), وی is an emphatic pronoun 'he' (referring back to the person named), and فرخ نژاد is a two-word adjectival epithet meaning 'of fortunate/bright lineage' (فرخ modifying نژاد). Together they report his repeated words.
segments.s734-g13: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'; شب 'night' is the time noun; کودک here qualifies شب (poetically 'the young/early night' or simply 'nightfall'); آمد is the past verb 'came/fell' used for nightfall; گذشته modifies the following numeral phrase as a participle 'having passed' (i.e., 'after having passed'); سه پاس literally 'three guards/watches'—'three watches' of the night. Together this sets the temporal background 'when night came and three watches had passed'.
segments.s734-g14: بیامد is the past form 'came' (here of the astrologer); بر + noun expresses motion toward/onto (بر خسرو = 'to Khosrow'); اخترشناس is an appositive noun 'the astrologer/astronomer'. The clause is a simple V + prepositional-object structure: 'the astrologer came to Khosrow.'
segments.s734-g15: از اخترشناسان is a prepositional object 'from the astrologers' (i.e., addressed to them). بپرسید is the past 3rd-singular 'asked' (شاه is the grammatical subject here, though its position after the verb is literary inversion). So the whole is 'the king asked of the astrologers' (i.e., 'the king asked the astrologers').
segments.s734-g16: که introduces an indirect question/clause 'that/what/who'; هرکس که 'whoever, each person' is a generalizing phrase; دارند is present/past-3rd-plural 'have/possess' here meaning 'is held by / has' (literal: 'have'); اختر نگاه is a genitive-like collocation 'the star's gaze' (اختر 'star' + نگاه 'gaze'). Together the clause asks, in effect, 'whoever it is that the star's gaze has' — i.e. 'who each person's fate/position is in the eyes of the stars.'
segments.s734-g57: This clause is an optative/negative wish: 'جز از شادمانیت هرگز مباد' literally ‘except from your joy, may there never be’. 'مباد' is an archaic/poetic optative meaning 'may it not be' (impersonal). 'شادمانیت' contains the possessive suffix -ت 'your' (‘your joy’), and the fronted 'جز از' ('except from') sets the one permitted source.
segments.s734-g58: 'ز گفتار ایشان مکن هیچ یاد' is an instruction: 'from their speech make no remembrance at all.' 'ز' = 'from/of', 'ایشان' = 'their', and 'مکن' is the negative imperative/formal prohibition 'do not'. 'هیچ یاد' = 'any remembrance/mention' (literally 'no/none memory'), so the verb 'مکن' takes that phrase as its object.
segments.s734-g59: The temporal clause 'ز موبد چو بشنید خسرو سخن' = 'when Khosrow heard speech from the mobed'. 'چو' here = 'when', 'بشنید' is simple past 'he heard', and 'سخن' is the object 'speech/words'. Word order is somewhat marked/poetic; the subject 'خسرو' is inserted after the verb.
segments.s734-g60: Main clause: 'بخندید و کاری نو افگند بن' = 'he laughed and cast/laid a new foundation (i.e. began a new undertaking)'. 'بخندید' is past tense 'he laughed'. 'کاری نو' = 'a new work/deed', and 'افگند بن' is a light-verb construction where 'افگند' (he cast/caused to fall) takes 'بن' (root/foundation) — idiomatically 'laid a foundation' or 'set a new enterprise in motion.'
segments.s734-g61: 'دبیر پسندیده را خواند پیش' = 'he called the chosen/approved secretary forward.' The adjective 'پسندیده' follows the noun (classical order). 'را' marks the definite object, 'خواند' here means 'summoned/called', and 'پیش' means 'into his presence/forward'.
segments.s734-g62: 'سخن گفت با او ز اندازه بیش' = 'he spoke with him about (that which was) beyond measure.' 'سخن گفت' is the verbal phrase 'spoke', 'با او' = 'with him', and the prepositional phrase 'ز اندازه بیش' (literally 'from/of measure more') is an idiom meaning 'in excess, beyond measure' — i.e. he discussed something excessive or beyond proper bounds.
segments.s734-g49: بیاورد is a 3rd-person singular past-form (classical narrative) meaning “brought.” Here the clause uses verb–subject order common in older Persian: بیاورد گنجور = “Ganjūr brought (it).” The conjunction و links the next short clause موبد بدید = “and the mōbed saw,” where بدید is likewise a past-tense form “saw.”
segments.s734-g50: دلش تنگ شد is an idiomatic past tense: دل (heart) + -ش (his) + تنگ شد “grew tight / grew sad.” شد is the past/simple perfective auxiliary ‘became.’ خامشی برگزید is a coordinated clause: خامشی (silence) is the noun object and برگزید is past ‘chose/selected,’ so literally “he chose silence.” The two clauses describe his internal reaction after the sight.
segments.s734-g51: ازان پس is a temporal phrase “after that/thereupon”; بدو is dative ‘to him’; گفت here functions as the reporting verb “said” (classical narrative past). The following clause is reported speech: یزدان بس است = “God is enough/sufficient,” where بس is an adjective ‘sufficient’ and است is the copula “is.”
segments.s734-g52: This is a rhetorical interrogative. کجا here functions as “where/how” in a rhetorical sense; برتر (‘superior’) is the predicate adjective placed before the source phrase از دانش (‘than knowledge’). هر کس is the indefinite subject “any person/anyone,” and است is the copula. Literally: “Where is anyone superior to knowledge?” (i.e. no one is above knowledge).
segments.s734-g53: گر is the conditional conjunction “if.” ایدون is an older/poetic word meaning roughly “now/if it comes to pass” (introducing the contingency), followed by که ‘that.’ ناچار here functions adverbially/qualifyingly as “inevitably/necessarily.” گردان سپهر is a nominative phrase ‘the revolving heavens/turning sky’ (گردان = revolving, سپهر = sky/heavens).
segments.s734-g54: دگرگون نماید combines دگرگون (changed/transformed) with نماید, a classical 3rd-person form used to express causation or potentiality (“may/might/causes to change”); together they render “should change / cause to be changed.” به marks the affected party (‘to/for’), جوینده چهر = “the seeker’s face” (جوینده = seeker, چهر = face/appearance). The clause thus says the heavens may alter the seeker’s appearance.
segments.s734-g55: به تیمار = ‘to (the) care/treatment’ (literally ‘to care’), کی is the interrogative ‘who’ (or rhetorically ‘who (then)’), باز indicates repetition ‘again,’ and گردد is a classical 3rd-person form meaning ‘returns / turns back’ (here used in the sense “will return”). ز is the poetic short form of از ‘from,’ and بد = ‘evil/misfortune.’ Together: “Who will again return to care after misfortune?”
segments.s734-g56: چنین گفته = ‘having said thus / to say such (a thing).’ از دانشی uses از in the sense ‘about/of’ (concerning knowledge). کی is again the interrogative ‘who,’ and سزد is the impersonal/present tense ‘it befits / is fitting’ (used idiomatically: “who is fit/worthy?”). So the clause questions the appropriateness of saying such things about knowledge: “Who is fit to have said such of knowledge?”
segments.s734-g33: پر اندیشه بد زان سخن شهریار = a nominal phrase where پر (prepositive adjective) means “full (of)”; اندیشه is the head noun “thought(s)”; بد qualifies it as “bad/evil.” زان links the phrase to what follows as “from/because of that,” and سخن شهریار is the genitive phrase “the king’s speech.” The whole segment means “full of ill thought because of the king’s words.”
segments.s734-g34: بران هفته کس را ندادند بار = بران (on/for that) + هفته used adverbially “for the week/for a week.” کس را is the definite object (کس = ‘anyone/someone’ with را marking the object). ندادند is past negative plural “they did not give/assign.” بار is used here as “burden/responsibility.” Together: “for that week they gave/assigned the burden to no one.”
segments.s734-g35: ز نخچیر و از می‌به یکسو کشید = two prepositional phrases (ز نخچیر “from the spoil/game” and از می‌به “from the wine” — می‌به is an archaic/poetic term for wine) joined by و. یکسو کشید is a verbal phrase with کشید (past of کشیدن) used intransitively/poetically “drew/moved to one side.” Overall: “he withdrew to one side from the spoil and from the wine.”
segments.s734-g36: بدان چندگه روی کس را ندید = بدان here (ب + آن) functions as ‘thereupon/for that (reason)’ or a discursive link; چندگه = “for a while.” روی کس را ندید is a regular S‑O‑V clause: روی “face” with کس را marked by را (object), and ندید is past negative “he did not see.” Together: “for that time/thereupon, for a while he saw no one’s face / saw no one.”
segments.s734-g37: همه مهتران سوی موبد شدند = همه مهتران “all the mahterān” (chiefs/elders; مهتران = the greater/older men) is the subject. سوی موبد = “toward the mobed (Zoroastrian priest).” شدند is past plural of شدن used here intransitively as ‘they went/arrived.’ The clause means “all the chiefs went to the mobed.”
segments.s734-g38: ز هر گونه‌ای داستانها زدند = ز هر گونه‌ای “of/from every kind” (i.e. of every sort). داستانها is the plural object “the stories/tales.” زدند here is a poetic use of the verb زدن (literally “they struck”) to mean “they uttered/performed/recited” — so “they told tales of every kind.”
segments.s734-g39: بدان تا چه بد نامور شاه را = بدان (be + آن) is a discourse verb form meaning “know/consider/attend” (introducing the informing clause). تا چه = “to what extent / how” (introduces degree). بد نامور is a compound adjective where بد negates or makes the fame negative, “ill‑famed/notorious.” شاه را (شاه marked with را) is the patient/topic of that evaluation. The whole means “know (then) how ill‑famed the king was.”
segments.s734-g40: که بربست بر کهتران راه را = که introduces a subordinate clause “that.” بربست is perfect/past “he bound/closed/sealed.” بر is the preposition “against/on/toward,” here “against.” کهتران means “the lesser/lowly people” (the plural). راه را is the object “the road” with را marking definiteness. Word order places the goal/recipient (بر کهتران) before the object (راه را) for emphasis: “that he shut the road against the lowly.”
segments.s734-g41: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause ('when'); بشنید is 3rd-person past 'he heard' and is the clause's verb; موبد is the subject (the mobed/priest). بشد (classical past of شد) here functions as a simple past resultative — 'came to be / arrived' — and نزد شاه is a prepositional phrase 'in the presence of the king' (نزد governs شاه). Word order is typical classical Persian: temporal clause + subject + verb + locative.
segments.s734-g42: بدو is the dative clitic phrase 'to him'; داد is the past 3rd-person of دادن 'gave'; یکسر is an adverb modifying the giving ('at once/entirely'); پیام is the direct object 'message'; سپاه is a genitive/qualifying noun 'of the army' (پیامِ سپاه). The phrase means he immediately handed over a report/message concerning the army.
segments.s734-g43: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' and داد is the past 'gave' (here 'gave an answer'). پاسخ is the noun 'answer' as the object; ورا is the dative 'to him'; شهریار (the subject) appears at the end in classical word order — literally, 'thus gave answer to him the king' = 'the king replied thus.'
segments.s734-g44: که introduces the content clause ('that'); من is the first‑person subject 'I'; تنگ‌دل is a compound adjective 'tang‑del' (literally 'narrow‑hearted' / 'sore‑hearted') functioning as a predicative; گشتم is 1st‑person past of گشتن 'I became'; از روزگار is a causal/prepositional phrase 'because of the times/fate' (از used as 'because of').
segments.s734-g45: ز is the poetic/prepositional form meaning 'from' or 'because of' that introduces the cause; گفتار is the noun 'speech/words'; این مرد اخترشناس is a noun phrase 'this man, an astrologer' that qualifies whose words they were (genitive relation). The whole phrase = 'because of the speech of this man, the astrologer'.
segments.s734-g46: Again ز = 'because of/from'; گردون is 'the sky/heavens' (often used for the celestial/fateful realm) and گردان is a postpositive adjective 'revolving' (the frequent epithet 'the ever‑turning heavens'); شدم is past 'I became'; ناسپاس is the predicate adjective 'ungrateful/thankless'. Together: 'because of the ever‑turning heavens I became ungrateful.'
segments.s734-g47: به + noun marks the indirect object or addressee: به گنجور 'to the treasurer/treasury'. گفت is the verb 'said' (here used with the following object); آن یکی پرنیان is the direct object phrase 'that one silken robe' (آن one + numeric/indefinite یکی + noun پرنیان). The clause is an order addressed to the treasurer.
segments.s734-g48: بیاور is the 2nd‑person imperative 'bring'; یکی رقعه is the object 'one sheet/a single note' (یکی used as 'one, a certain'); اندر میان is a locative phrase 'within/inside' (اندر = in, میان = midst/inside) indicating where the sheet should go (i.e. put the note inside the robe). The structure is imperative + object + locative.
segments.s734-g25: دل شاه غمگین شد is a simple past copular clause: دل شاه (the king's heart) is the possessed noun + possessor in the regular Persian order; غمگین شد uses شد ‘became’ (past) to mean 'grew sad'. از کارشان is a causal prepositional phrase (از = 'because of') with شان being the 3rd‑person plural possessive suffix 'their' attached to کار.
segments.s734-g26: وزان is a contracted poetic sequence (و + از + آن) meaning 'and from that' or 'and because of that'; ناسزاوار qualifies گفتارشان, forming the compound sense 'offensive/insulting speech'. گفتارشان is the noun 'speech' with the 3rd‑person plural possessive suffix -شان.
segments.s734-g27: چنین گفت is a formulaic 'thus said' (چنین 'thus' + گفت 'said'); با مرد داننده is a prepositional phrase 'to/with the knowing man' (با + noun + adjective), and شاه placed after it is the agent ('the king said')—a poetic inversion in which the speaker appears after the verb and object phrase.
segments.s734-g28: که introduces the content clause 'that...'; نیکو کنِید uses نیکو as an adverb/adjective 'well/virtuously' and کنِید is the 2nd‑person plural subjunctive/imperative form of کردن used here as a polite command 'do/act/look (well)'. اندر اختر نگاه is a locative phrase 'into/upon the star' (اختر used metaphorically as 'the exalted/bright one') with نگاه 'gaze' as the noun object.
segments.s734-g29: نگر is an imperative 'beware/attend'; تا introduces a negative purpose here ('so that...not' / 'lest') and governs the following negative subjunctive نگردد ('may not become/turn'). زبانتان is the possessed noun 'your speech/words' and برین is the fused form of بر + این ('against/on this' or 'to this'), so together 'so that your tongue does not turn to this'.
segments.s734-g30: به پیش is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in the presence of' or 'before', followed by بزرگان 'the great ones' (plural) and ایران‌زمین 'the land of Iran' as the specifying genitive; this phrase indicates the audience/location before whom the tongue must not turn.
segments.s734-g31: همی‌داشت is the imperfect/continuous past auxiliary ('kept' / 'was keeping'); آن اختران را is the definite object ('those stars' with آن + plural) marked by the object marker را; نگاه is the noun 'gaze' and here functions as the thing he 'kept'—'he kept his gaze on those stars.'
segments.s734-g32: نهاده is a past participle 'having placed/put', followed by بران (poetic contraction of بر آن, 'upon that') and بسته as an adjective 'bound/fastened'; بر مهر شاه is a prepositional phrase 'on/upon the king's مهر', where مهر here functions as a possessed noun ('seal' or 'royal mark'). The whole cluster modifies the prior clause: 'having placed (them) bound upon the king's seal.'
segments.s734-g17: بدیدی is an archaic/past-form of دیدی (literally 'did you see' / 'do you know?') and begins the clause; که introduces the subordinate content clause. فرجام این کار چیست is a nominal clause ending with the copula‑question چیست ('what is ...?'), where چی (what) precedes the copula (typical in Classical Persian questions). The whole segment asks for the outcome of the affair.
segments.s734-g18: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; زیچ is an archaic/obscure term here (not the modern instrument), functioning as part of a compound with اختر meaning a star/constellation. این جهاندار is the noun phrase 'this sovereign/ruler' and the segment ends with چیست (what is) — the question asks 'from which star/position does this ruler come?'. Word order places the interrogative چی before the copula ست.
segments.s734-g19: چنین ('thus') + داد (past 'gave') introduce the reported reply; پاسخ ('answer') is the direct object and ستاره شمر ('the star Shamr') is the agent placed after the verb — verb‑initial order is common in classical/poetic Persian. داد is a simple past (he gave/uttered).
segments.s734-g20: که introduces the content of the reply. بر چرخ گردان is a prepositional phrase 'on/upon the revolving wheel/heavens' where گردان is an adjective 'revolving'. نیابی is a present/subjunctive/negative form of یافتن (here morphological marking indicates 'you will not find' / 'you do not find'); گذر ('passage') is the noun referred to by the verb. Word order places the verb before its noun (poetic inversion): 'you will not find passage on the turning heavens.'
segments.s734-g21: ازین (از + ین, the poetic/orthographic form of 'از این') means 'from this'; کودک is the noun 'child/young one' and آشوب گیرد is verb phrase 'turmoil will seize' where گیرد is 3rd‑person singular present/future 'will take/seize'. زمین is the affected noun 'the earth/land'. The phrase thus means 'from this child a turmoil will seize the land' (cause + result order is fronted).
segments.s734-g22: نخواند here is the verb 'call/summon' (in context with negative sense 'will not summon'); سپاهت is 'your army' (سپاه + enclitic ـت 'your'). برو is an imperative 'go'. آفرین can be an interjection 'bravo/praise' (here an exclamatory tag). The clause sequence is compressed/elliptical: 'he will not summon your army — go, Afarin!' (the last word is an emphatic exclamation and its exact pragmatic role is poetic/ambiguous).
segments.s734-g23: هم 'also/likewise' modifies the clause; از راه یزدان is a prepositional phrase 'from the way/path of God' and بگردد is a verb meaning 'will turn/turn away' (third person, classical subjunctive/future sense). به نیز 'also/in addition' reinforces the adverbial sense — the segment says that the matter will likewise turn away from God's path.
segments.s734-g24: ازین بیشتر 'any more than this' is a phrase meaning 'beyond this'; چون here is 'how' (introducing a rhetorical question) and سراییم is first‑person plural/subjunctive of سرودن/سراییدن 'to compose/sing' (classical poetic form 'how shall we compose'). چیز 'thing/matter' is the object. Together: 'How shall we compose anything more of this matter?'
segments.s734-g1: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بر + پادشاهی + -ش is a prepositional phrase literally 'upon his kingship' (پادشاهیش = پادشاهی + enclitic -ش 'his'). شد is past of شدن 'became/reached' and combines with the duration پنج‌سال ('five years') to mean 'his reign reached five years.' Word order is chronological: temporal conjunction → phrase marking domain of the duration → verb → duration.
segments.s734-g2: به گیتی = 'in the world' (به used in the sense 'in/within' with گیتی 'world'). نبودش is the negative past 'there was not/was not there' with the enclitic -ش referring back to the person; سراسر modifies the following noun 'throughout/entire'. همال, here used idiomatically, means 'a match/peer' — the whole clause says 'there was no peer/equal throughout the world.'
segments.s734-g3: ششم سال = 'the sixth year' (ordinal + noun). زان = 'from that/after that' (ز + آن), introducing the reference point 'that daughter of Caesar' (زان دخت قیصر = 'after that daughter of the Caesar'). چو ماه is a simile 'like the moon' that prepares the comparison applied to the coming child. The phrase order is typical older Persian: time → reference → simile marker → simile noun.
segments.s734-g4: یکی here functions as 'a certain' / 'one (someone)'. پورش is a proper name (Pursh) and آمد is the past 'came/appeared' (used also for a birth/appearance). همانند شاه 'resembling a king' is a simile phrase modifying the newborn; Persian here places the verb before the simile phrase. The whole unit reads 'a certain Pursh came, like a king.'
segments.s734-g5: نبود is the negative past 'there was not' (نبود = 'was not'). آن زمان 'that time' specifies the era. رسم بانگ نماز is a nominal phrase: رسم 'custom/rite', بانگ 'sound/cry' + نماز 'prayer' — together 'the custom of the prayer-call'. The clause states that such a custom did not exist then.
segments.s734-g6: به گوش = 'to the ear' (به used as 'to/for' here). چنان 'so/thus' modifies پروریده 'nurtured/cultivated' (a participial/adjectival form). بناز is a present or poetic imperfective meaning 'it boasts/is proud' (here describing the sound's effect on the ear). The sequence describes how the sound was fashioned to please/pride the ear.
segments.s734-g7: یکی نام گفتی = 'you (at one time) spoke/said one name' (یکی as 'one/a certain'). مر = 'to me' (old pronominal clitic meaning 'me' or 'for me'), او = 'him', را is the definite-object marker, and پدر is the name/title 'father'. Grammatically this is an object clause: 'you said one name to me for him:
segments.s734-g8: نهانی دگر آشکارا دگر is a compact adverbial contrast: نهانی 'secretly' (adverb) + دگر 'another/else' (meaning 'one way'), آشکارا 'openly' + دگر 'another' (meaning 'another way'). Together: 'once secretly, another time openly.' The two adverbials are parallel and contrastive.

sentences.s734-s5: He would whisper words secretly into the inner ear and would recite them openly outside.
sentences.s734-s6: Khosrow Qobad spoke into the inner ear and kept saying, 'A lion is he, of fortunate lineage.'
sentences.s734-s7: When night fell and three watches had passed, the astrologer came to Khosrow.
sentences.s734-s8: The king asked the astrologers who, under the star's gaze, each person was.
sentences.s734-s29: Let there never be any remembrance except from your joy; do not recall their words at all.
sentences.s734-s30: When Khosrow heard the mobed's words, he laughed and set a new enterprise in motion (literally: cast a new foundation).
sentences.s734-s31: He called the chosen secretary forward; he spoke with him about something beyond measure.
sentences.s734-s25: Ganjūr brought it and the mōbed saw; his heart grew tight, and he chose silence.
sentences.s734-s26: After that he said to him, “God is sufficient; where is anyone superior to knowledge?”
sentences.s734-s27: If now it should happen that, inevitably, the revolving heavens turn, and the sky be changed before the seeker’s face, ...
sentences.s734-s28: Who will return to care after misfortune? To have said such a thing of knowledge — who is fit to do that?
sentences.s734-s17: Filled with evil thoughts because of the king's words, they did not entrust the burden to anyone for that week.
sentences.s734-s18: He drew himself aside from the spoil and from the wine, and for a while saw no one there.
sentences.s734-s19: All the chiefs went to the mobed, and from every kind they recounted tales.
sentences.s734-s20: So that you may know how ill‑famed the king had become: he had shut the road against the lowly.
sentences.s734-s21: When the mobed heard, he went up to the king and at once gave him a message about the army.
sentences.s734-s22: Thus the king gave him this reply: 'I have grown narrow‑hearted because of the times.'
sentences.s734-s23: Because of this astrologer's words I have become ungrateful toward the heavens.
sentences.s734-s24: He said to the treasurer, 'Bring a silken robe—bring one sheet (a letter) inside it.'
sentences.s734-s13: The king's heart grew sad because of their deeds, and because of that foulness of their speech.
sentences.s734-s14: Thus the king spoke to the knowing man: 'Set your good gaze upon the high one.'
sentences.s734-s15: Be careful so that your tongue does not turn to this before the great men of the land of Iran.
sentences.s734-s16: He kept his gaze fixed on those stars, having placed them bound upon the king's seal.
sentences.s734-s9: Do you see what the outcome of this affair is, and from which star this sovereign comes?
sentences.s734-s10: The star Shamr answered thus: 'On the turning heavens you will find no passage.'
sentences.s734-s11: From this child turmoil will seize the earth; he will not summon your army — 'Afarin, go!'
sentences.s734-s12: Moreover it will turn away from God's path as well; how shall we compose any more of this matter?
sentences.s734-s1: When his kingship reached five years, there was no equal to him throughout the world.
sentences.s734-s2: In the sixth year after that daughter of Caesar, a son named Pursh came, like the moon and like a king.
sentences.s734-s3: At that time there was no custom of the call to prayer; to the ear it was so cultivated that it exulted.
sentences.s734-s4: You once gave him a name — 'father' — sometimes secretly, sometimes openly.
